subsystem: FastCGITransport::onHeader — the per-parameter callback in HHVM's (HipHop Virtual Machine) FastCGI server transport. It receives one FastCGI FCGI_PARAMS name/value pair (a CGI meta-variable such as HTTP_HOST, REQUEST_METHOD, or a client-supplied HTTP_* header) as two folly::IOBuf chains, materialises them into std::strings, applies the httpoxy mitigation, and records the pair in the request's parameter map that later becomes the script's $_SERVER / environment.
provenance: Identified from the fully-qualified method name FastCGITransport::onHeader and the FastCGI wire types: folly::IOBuf and folly::io::Cursor (readFixedString / the .get() chain API) are Facebook's folly library, and FastCGITransport is HHVM's runtime/server/fastcgi transport. The banner comment 'sample: primevul_train_2071_fixed' and the file name train_0088_cwe665.cpp mark it as a PrimeVul-derived sample. The inline comment on lines 7-8 ('Don't allow requests to inject an HTTP_PROXY environment variable by sending a Proxy header') is the textbook description of the httpoxy fix.
trust_boundary: onHeader is driven by the FastCGI record parser: key_chain and value_chain hold raw bytes read off the FastCGI socket, i.e. attacker-influenced wire data. In the normal deployment a trusted front-end web server (nginx/apache) speaks FastCGI to HHVM, but the FCGI_PARAMS it forwards include CGI variables derived from client-controlled HTTP request headers — in particular the 'Proxy:' request header is mapped to the HTTP_PROXY CGI variable. So even with a trusted front-end, the NAME and VALUE bytes reaching onHeader are partly client-controlled; both the length prefixes (via computeChainDataLength) and the raw name/value bytes are hostile. If the FastCGI port is reachable directly, every parameter name and value is fully attacker-controlled.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point for a single FastCGI parameter (name/value) pair. It takes ownership (std::unique_ptr) of two folly::IOBuf chains: key_chain holds the parameter NAME bytes and value_chain holds the VALUE bytes, exactly as they arrived on the FastCGI record stream. An IOBuf is a linked chain of buffer segments, so a single logical parameter may be spread across several IOBuf nodes. Taking the buffers by unique_ptr means onHeader is responsible for their lifetime and is the choke point through which every FastCGI parameter must pass — which is precisely why the httpoxy filter lives here.

A folly Cursor is constructed over the raw key_chain to read across its segments. key_chain->computeChainDataLength() sums the byte length of every segment in the chain and returns a size_t — this is the attacker-influenced length of the parameter name. readFixedString(n) then allocates and copies exactly n bytes out of the cursor into a std::string named key. Because it is a byte-count-driven fixed-length read (not a C-string read), key can legally contain embedded NUL bytes and any binary content; its length is whatever the wire said it was.

This is the httpoxy mitigation. strcasecmp performs a case-insensitive comparison between the parameter name (via key.c_str(), a NUL-terminated C-string view of the std::string) and the literal "HTTP_PROXY". If they match, the function returns immediately WITHOUT storing the parameter, so the malicious HTTP_PROXY meta-variable never reaches the request map or the child script's environment. The case-insensitive compare is deliberate: it catches Http_Proxy, http_proxy, etc. This blocks CVE-2016-1000109 (the HHVM instance of the httpoxy family), where a client 'Proxy:' request header is translated by the web server into an HTTP_PROXY CGI variable that outbound-HTTP libraries then honour as a proxy address.

Symmetric to the key read: a Cursor is built over value_chain, its total chained length is computed with computeChainDataLength() (again an attacker-influenced size_t), and readFixedString copies exactly that many bytes into the std::string value. This runs only for parameters that survived the HTTP_PROXY check on line 9. As with the key, value is a length-delimited binary string and may hold NULs or arbitrary bytes.

The surviving name/value pair is inserted into the member map m_requestParams (an associative container keyed by the parameter name). This map is the accumulated CGI parameter set for the current request and is later exposed to the executing script as $_SERVER and, for the affected variables, the process environment. operator[] inserts-or-overwrites, so a repeated key silently replaces the earlier value with the last one seen. After the insert the function returns and the two IOBuf chains are freed with their unique_ptrs.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This single hard-coded blocklist entry is the entire httpoxy defense, so its safety depends on two contracts the implementation must uphold. (1) onHeader must be the ONLY path by which a FastCGI parameter reaches m_requestParams / the child environment — if any other code path (a different record type, a bulk copy, or a pre-populated environment) can set HTTP_PROXY, the filter is bypassed. (2) The exact byte string tested here must equal the name the downstream environment builder and any outbound-HTTP client (which calls getenv("HTTP_PROXY") / getenv("http_proxy")) will look up. The check covers the upper-case HTTP_PROXY that the CGI convention produces and is case-insensitive, which is correct for this variable; but it is a denylist of exactly one string, so it is inherently fragile to any future or alternate name (e.g. a lower-case 'http_proxy' passed through verbatim by a non-conforming front-end would be stored, since CGI normally upper-cases but this code never normalises). If the invariant 'the front-end always presents this variable as HTTP_PROXY' fails, the mitigation misses it. This is the CWE-665 essence: an untrusted request header improperly initialising a security-sensitive environment variable used to configure outbound connections.

The comparison operates on key.c_str(), a NUL-terminated C-string, whereas key was produced by readFixedString as a length-delimited std::string that may contain embedded NUL bytes, and the value stored on line 14 (m_requestParams[key]) uses the FULL binary std::string. strcasecmp therefore compares only the bytes up to the first NUL, while storage keeps every byte — a validation-view vs use-view mismatch (CWE-158 / CWE-20). Concretely: for a key like "HTTP_PROXY\0extra", c_str() truncates to "HTTP_PROXY" and the request is (correctly) blocked; for "HTTP_PROXYx" it is not equal and is stored, but the resulting env name is HTTP_PROXYx which getenv("HTTP_PROXY") will not match — so no concrete bypass to inject exactly HTTP_PROXY exists in THIS check. The finding is that comparing a filtered C-string while persisting the full binary string is a fragile pattern: it only stays safe because the sensitive name has no NUL-prefix that both matches the filter target and the downstream lookup. Any additional denylist entry, or a downstream consumer that keys on the full std::string, would be bypassable via an embedded NUL. The implementation must key its filter on the same byte string it stores.

computeChainDataLength() returns an attacker-influenced size_t and is passed straight to readFixedString, which allocates a std::string of that size; the same pattern repeats for the value on line 12. There is no per-parameter or per-request cap applied here. FastCGI name/value lengths are length-prefixed (up to a 4-byte, ~2^31 length), so a malicious or compromised front-end can declare and stream very large parameter names/values, forcing large heap allocations and, across many parameters/requests, memory-exhaustion denial of service (CWE-770 / CWE-789). The allocation size is bounded by the bytes actually buffered, so this is throttled by how much the peer sends rather than a pure size-field lie, which lowers severity — but the code applies no defensive ceiling of its own and relies entirely on upstream record-size limits. The implementation must enforce a maximum parameter and total-header size before materialising these strings.

Every parameter name except the single HTTP_PROXY case is accepted verbatim and written into the request parameter map that becomes $_SERVER / the CGI environment. Sensitive CGI meta-variables (AUTH_TYPE, REMOTE_USER, SCRIPT_FILENAME, CONTENT_LENGTH, PATH_TRANSLATED, etc.) are NOT filtered here — which is correct ONLY if the FastCGI socket is exclusively reachable by a trusted front-end that sets those variables authoritatively (CWE-20 trust-boundary assumption). If the FastCGI listener is exposed to untrusted clients, an attacker can spoof arbitrary CGI variables and application state. Additionally, operator[] overwrites on a duplicate key, so if the front-end (or an attacker who can inject a second parameter with the same name) sends the same variable twice, the last occurrence silently wins — a header-smuggling / precedence hazard if different layers pick a different occurrence. The implementation must (a) restrict who may connect to the FastCGI endpoint and (b) define a deterministic policy for duplicate parameter names.

summary: This is the FIXED httpoxy path in HHVM's FastCGITransport::onHeader: it correctly drops the attacker-influenced HTTP_PROXY FastCGI parameter (CVE-2016-1000109 family) with a case-insensitive check before storing any other name/value pair. The core fix is sound and no concrete bypass to inject exactly HTTP_PROXY was found. Residual, mostly latent risks are defense-in-depth: the mitigation is a one-entry denylist, the filter compares a NUL-truncated c_str() while storing the full binary key, the two readFixedString calls allocate on an uncapped attacker-influenced length, and every other CGI variable is trusted to the front-end.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Sole choke point for FastCGI parameters carrying attacker-influenced wire bytes; its position is load-bearing for the httpoxy filter (trust-boundary finding).
Cursor construction over a live, owned IOBuf; no length or lifetime contract violated on its own.
Attacker-influenced size_t length passed straight into an allocation with no cap (unbounded-allocation finding).
Materialises the length-delimited binary strings that later feed both the filter and the environment; allocation size is attacker-influenced.
Single-string case-insensitive denylist AND a c_str()/NUL vs full-binary-key view mismatch — two distinct findings anchored here.
c_str() is used within the same statement while the local std::string key is alive and unmodified — no dangling pointer (CWE-416 not applicable).
Correct fail-closed behaviour: the parameter is dropped and never stored; no leak or state corruption.
Stores every non-HTTP_PROXY name verbatim (arbitrary CGI-variable trust assumption) and overwrites on duplicate keys.

findings.title: httpoxy mitigation is a single-entry denylist at one choke point
findings.bug_class: Improper initialization of a security-sensitive environment variable from an untrusted request header (httpoxy)
findings.invariant: onHeader must be the ONLY path that populates the request/environment parameter set, and the exact byte string filtered here must equal the name every downstream environment builder and outbound-HTTP client (getenv("HTTP_PROXY")/"http_proxy") resolves, for all case/normalisation variants the front-end may emit.
findings.failure_mode: A client sends a 'Proxy:' HTTP header; the front-end maps it to the HTTP_PROXY CGI variable and forwards it over FastCGI. This check drops it. But if any other code path sets HTTP_PROXY, or a non-conforming front-end presents the name in a form this literal compare misses (e.g. an un-upper-cased 'http_proxy' that CGI normally normalises but this code does not), the variable reaches outbound HTTP libraries and redirects server-side requests through an attacker proxy (SSRF/credential capture).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no other code writes to m_requestParams / the child environment for a proxy-influencing name, and that parameter names are normalised (upper-cased) before this compare or that only HTTP_PROXY can ever appear.

findings.title: Filter compares NUL-truncated c_str() while storing the full binary key
findings.bug_class: Validation-view vs storage-view mismatch (embedded NUL truncation)
findings.invariant: The denylist must be applied to the same byte sequence that is persisted; a filter must not evaluate a NUL-truncated view of a key that is stored (and later consumed) as a full binary string.
findings.failure_mode: key is a length-delimited std::string that may contain embedded NULs, but strcasecmp only inspects bytes before the first NUL while m_requestParams[key] stores all bytes. No concrete bypass to inject exactly HTTP_PROXY exists here (a NUL-prefixed 'HTTP_PROXY\0x' is still caught, and 'HTTP_PROXYx' does not resolve to HTTP_PROXY downstream), so impact is limited — but the pattern is fragile: any added denylist entry, or a downstream consumer keyed on the full std::string, becomes bypassable via an embedded NUL.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify how the parameter name is turned into an environment variable name downstream (c_str vs full string) and whether embedded NULs in FCGI_PARAMS names are rejected before reaching onHeader.

findings.title: Uncapped allocation from attacker-influenced chain length
findings.invariant: A maximum per-parameter and total FCGI_PARAMS size must be enforced before materialising the name/value into std::strings.
findings.failure_mode: computeChainDataLength() returns an attacker-influenced size_t (FastCGI name/value lengths are length-prefixed up to ~2^31) and is passed straight to readFixedString for both key (line 5) and value (line 12) with no cap in this code. A malicious/compromised front-end streaming very large parameters, or many of them, forces large heap allocations and memory-exhaustion DoS. Severity is reduced because the allocation is bounded by bytes actually sent, so it relies on upstream record-size limits rather than a size-field lie.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the FastCGI record/params parser enforces a hard ceiling on individual parameter length and cumulative header size before onHeader is invoked.

findings.title: All non-HTTP_PROXY CGI variables trusted verbatim; duplicate keys overwrite
findings.invariant: The FastCGI endpoint must be reachable only by a trusted front-end that sets authoritative CGI meta-variables, and a deterministic policy must govern duplicate parameter names.
findings.failure_mode: Only HTTP_PROXY is filtered; every other name (AUTH_TYPE, REMOTE_USER, SCRIPT_FILENAME, CONTENT_LENGTH, PATH_TRANSLATED, ...) is stored verbatim. If the FastCGI socket is exposed to untrusted clients, an attacker can spoof any CGI variable and application/security state. Because operator[] overwrites, a duplicated parameter name silently keeps the last occurrence, enabling precedence/smuggling confusion if different layers read different occurrences.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the FastCGI listener is bound to trusted transports only (localhost/socket perms) and how duplicate FCGI_PARAMS names are expected to resolve.

audit_checklist: Prove onHeader is the sole writer of m_requestParams / the child environment, so no path sets HTTP_PROXY around the line-9 filter.
Confirm parameter names are normalised (upper-cased) or that only the exact CGI form HTTP_PROXY can ever reach this compare; consider matching http_proxy too for defense in depth.
Check how m_requestParams keys become environment variable names downstream (c_str vs full std::string) and whether embedded NULs in names are rejected upstream.
Verify a hard cap on individual FCGI_PARAMS name/value length and on total header size before readFixedString allocates.
Confirm the FastCGI endpoint is restricted to trusted front-ends (bind address / unix-socket permissions), since only HTTP_PROXY is filtered.
Define and verify the duplicate-parameter-name policy given operator[] last-write-wins semantics.
